title: Moving to Mac
preview: Today, I purchased my first MacBook. For the past 23 years, I've been an avid Windows lover. Always. In fact, I have loved Windows so much that I spent much of the free time of my...
content:
Today, I purchased my first MacBook. For the past 23 years, I've been an avid Windows lover. Always. In fact, I have loved Windows so much that I spent much of the free time of my youth being an aggressive Mac hater.

What drew me to Windows, I don't know if I can fully articulate. Perhaps it was the intuitive UI and UX, the way things were organized, or simply the way the operating system evolved over time. Whatever the reason, it felt like home.

But life has a way of presenting the unexpected, and I've found myself standing at the intersection of idealism and sustainibility. Between dentistry, writing, software projects, research, and the growing number of pursuits competing for my attention, I've come to realize that a MacBook better serves the person I am becoming.

So, I move forward.

I step into the Mac ecosystem, not because I have stopped loving Windows, but because I've come to learn that admiration alone is not enough to justify staying. Sometimes, growth requires us to choose what best supports our future over what feels most familiar in our past.

While my path now leads elsewhere, I will continue to watch the the evolution of the Windows OS from afar with the same affection from the very first time I interacted with it, hoping to witness and celebrate its growth in all the ways I know it is capable of achieving.

After all, first loves have a way of staying with us, even when they are no longer where we belong.

title: Roach Infestation
preview: A year ago, my apartment had a roach infestation. To my growing horror, I discovered an increasing number of baby roaches absent-mindedly scuttling their way across my wooden floo...
content:
A year ago, my apartment had a roach infestation. To my growing horror, I discovered an increasing number of baby roaches absent-mindedly scuttling their way across my wooden floors. A few clues pointed me to the potential location of the roach spawner. First, there was increase in traffic congestion around the cabinet underneath the kitchen sink. Second, the habitat in that very cabinet was prime real estate for a small roach family to invest in a little home. Turns out, this city does not accommodate unauthorized tenants, so the choice was theirs: leave or die.

The roaches banded forces and decided to defend their territory. Little did they know, the switch had already been flipped. Defensive tactics had now turned offensive, and extermination was in order. The first strike was to snipe on sight. Every brave roach who dared enter the visual plane would be executed with deadly precision. Failure of this tactic quickly ensued due to the unforseen crippling fear of the snipee at executing such commands.

A new strategy developed: infiltrate from the inside. Traps were set and bait was placed. All that was left was to wait and watch. The poison slowly infiltrated the colony in a course of months. To my excitement, the numbers began dwindle.

The next phase consisted of the tedious removal the affected bodies. First, I began with the easier, less affected regions of the kitchen, emptying their belongings and wiping away any last traces of the wounded soldiers. Finally, I made my way to the dreaded sink cabinet, only to realize that while it was a prominent outpost, it was not the main base.

Over months of deep cleaning, searching, and scavenging, the true source finally revealed itself when moving my fridge. At last, the stronghold was found. There, clustered under the warmth of the fridge compressor, lay the remnants of the colony, already seemingly affected by the poisonous bait released months earlier.

Today, the bait remains in place for any stragglers who choose to pass by, a symbol of their fate if they choose to attempt unauthorized settlement.


All jokes aside, this invasion was a profound experience largely because of its timing within my own healing journey. Through this experience, I found that the strategy for healing was remarkably similar to that of exterminating the roach colony.

At times, roaches would randomly appear, much like unhealthy habits, intrusive thoughts, and coping mechanisms. My first instinct in both cases was to eliminate the visual problem immediately, but much like my experience with the roaches, sniping on sight resulted in extraordinary failure as it never really targeted the root cause. Instead, I had to allow the roaches to live long enough for the poison to take affect. Likewise, I had to spend a lot of time allowing unsightly parts of myself to exist while God quietely worked underneath the surface.

I also noticed how both events unfolded over the course of months. Although some days were definitely more eventful than others, neither event was some dramatic breakthrough. Progress came through taking small actions each day and addressing one layer at a time which slowly built up my tolerance for handling the creatures.

When the stronghold was finally revealed underneath the refrigerator, the battle had already been won. The colony lay on its dying breath through the work of the poison, and all that was left for me to do was to clean it up. Looking back, I suspect that if I had found the stronghold earlier, I would likely have been paralyzed by fear and unable to continue forward. Instead, encountering it after months of gradual preparation gave me the strength to handle the remains by the time I reached the true source. In that same vein, I find that healing reveals deeper wounds only after we have built the capacity to face them.

Today, the bait remains in place. Not because the colony is expected to return, but because maintenance is a part of the long-term solution. The work may be done, but the practices that made it possible remain to protect against future undesired invasions. I am deeply amused at the timing of these events and how they have supported my personal growth. In my story, roaches helped me understand the principles of healing. What it will be for you, I have no idea. Regardless, I do think it is worth paying attention to what and how things happen around us, because perhaps then, you may stumble upon more of life's secrets.

title: VORTEX SHEDDING
preview: Today, I had two kayaks strapped to the top of my car, remnants of the weekend trek to Stallings Island to go find and feed the mythic donkeys. As I turned onto an interstate road...
content:
Today, I had two kayaks strapped to the top of my car, remnants of the weekend trek to Stallings Island to go find and feed the mythic donkeys. As I turned onto an interstate road, I was faced with an unforeseen challenger, a 55 mph speed limit.

I figured the straps would hold and pressed my foot cautiously into the accelerator, watching the tip of the kayaks from the windscreen. They remained in place, but a loud groan began to perpetuate outside my window.

These signals triggered a commencement of mini experiments. First, I began with acceleration and deceleration. As expected, the groans matched the velocity of the car. Maybe the straps were just loose? I tugged on them overhead, but instead of resolving the groans, I heard an increase in pitch. Curious. Reaching outside the window, I grasped the vibrating strap. Notably, the groans muted as energy shifted into my hand.

I finally decided it was probably a good idea for me to stop. Pulling into a gas station, I whipped out my phone and asked Google’s Gemini about the phenomenon of groaning kayak straps. It quickly responded with a term called “vortex shedding” and recommended twisting the parts of the strap not directly touching the kayaks. With a quick confirmation from a trusty source (Reddit), I put in a few twists and proceeded back to the interstate road. To my delight, the suggestion worked and I was met with the absence of the groaning vibrations.

Later that day, I began my spiral down vortex shedding, a phenomenon (probably well understood by aerospace nerds) where wind passing by flat straps induces vibrations. As air is separated by the straps, whirlpools of low pressure form on each side of the strap in an alternating fashion leading to a high-speed vibration culminating in acoustic resonance. According to Gemini, “every taut object has a natural frequency at which it wants to vibrate when disturbed (like a tuned guitar string) and when these frequencies align (frequency of shedding vortices and frequency of the strap), resonance occurs.”

What's interesting to me was not necessarily the physics itself, but the idea that resonance requires structure. It reminded me of a recent encounter I had with someone whose mind operated much like my own. When two people of the same frequency connect, they have the capacity to build resonance. However, much like the strap, if done at such uncontrolled intensity for prolonged periods of time, it may result in irrecoverable damage. Thus, resonance, something that can produce sound in a strap and love between two individuals, must be bounded and contained to preserve the integrity of both interacting structures. On another note, one can refuse to embody the fullness of who they are meant to be as an independent entity. An identity remains unexplored. The strap remains slack. It is indeed safe. But it comes at the cost of never truly producing resonance.
